item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2852 was the article that really cemented my SCP fandom into being more than just a passing interest, and it was heightened by the fact that my grandfather died the day after I read it, and his funeral was a few days after that. My family is Italian-American, and while my parents raised us in Virginia, the rest of the family still lived in South Jersey…..in super Italian, Catholic, South Jersey. This would be like if read SCP-4666 right before you left for a Christmas vacation in Canada. So having Cousin Johnny fresh in my mind as I witnessed all the pointless ceremony, artifacts, and procedural grief while family I rarely see all gathered together… getting conscripted into casket carrying with my cousins, seeing relatives who grew up and stayed in the same town all their lives… just made the icky-ness of that article feel much more real to me. Later when I read Imago, I loved the concept even more. The idea of alien entity looking at Christianity and thinking: 'Death, rebirth, and ceremony, sure, I can do that' is great.

So while Cousin Johnny is great avatar for cicada god shenanigans when it interacts with Catholic and Anglican churches, and their obsession with ceremony, symbolism, and physical participation, it would make sense that It's influence would look different in American Protestant churches. The prosperity gospel is having a resurgence here, but really, it's always been in this country. In short, the concept in Calvinism that we are all predetermined to go to heaven or hell already, and that if you are going to heaven then obviously God would make you wealthy and successful, has been a blight on our nation's psyche since before we were a nation, as many of the settlers, pilgrims included, were fans of Calvinist philosophy. The active preaching of Calvinist theology has waxed and waned over the last two centuries, but it's influence is constantly echoed in most Americans: this entanglement of monetary desire with prayer and faith.

So when the cicada god spreads it's influence throughout the rest of America, what else would it latch onto but the prosperity gospel?

Gaaah, I loved this one.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

The crosslinks already in the article to SCP-2852 and SCP-3004 do a really good job linking this article out, so there is not much left for Wikiwalk to do here. However, the references to MTF Mu-4 and MTF Gamma-5 could easily link to their respective spots on the MTF page, and from there to other SCPs related to that task force. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

A Foundation-operated bot (I/O-SILVER) is to monitor online communities for markers associated with SCP-3089. Should a community infected with SCP-3089 be identified, a joint operation involving MTF Mu-4 ("Debuggers"), MTF Nu-13 ("Rasczak's Roughnecks"), and MTF Gamma-5 ("Red Herrings") is to be conducted to contain all members of this community and remove all evidence of its existence from the public record.

To this:

A Foundation-operated bot (I/O-SILVER) is to monitor online communities for markers associated with SCP-3089. Should a community infected with SCP-3089 be identified, a joint operation involving MTF Mu-4 ("Debuggers"), MTF Nu-13 ("Rasczak's Roughnecks"), and MTF Gamma-5 ("Red Herrings") is to be conducted to contain all members of this community and remove all evidence of its existence from the public record.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes when I review stuff in draft, I can get too attached to what I think the end product should be. With a bit of distance, I can appreciate what this is - some beautifully skewed mimicry used to good effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …this is my new favourite MTF…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate it; it took me a while to feel good about this skip, but I do now. xD

Also, if it helps: MTF Pi-8 ("Playa Hataz") is, in my mind, an MTF dedicated solely to investigating, counteracting, and containing anomalous PUAs. I imagine they consist largely of women with military experience and a shit-ton of anti-PUA-anomaly countermeasures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good skip and you should feel good about it. +1.

Also, the whole concept of PUAs using anomalies makes me want to go scrub myself raw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The original 3004 kinda lost me for various reasons I won't spam out here but this one I like. Takes the concept of a spontaneous bizarre and dangerous religion springing up out of seemingly nowhere and goes to interesting places with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the Cicada-Pope Cicada-Catholic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this related to Cousin Johnny at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One iteration (that I never posted) made reference to that (Abraham and Isaac), but — tbh, as a non-Christian, I wasn't sure how well known that story is. That is a totally valid alternate explanation, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
lambs slaughtered to feed the lion, the Son (Jesus) slaughtered to feed <blank>

I read this as the Son being Isaac. Abraham (nearly) sacrificing his Son to God a very well known Biblical story, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am super-curious as to your thoughts (and I'm also super-glad that you liked this!). Also, yes, cicadas freak me way the fuck out — despite the fact that they're really among the least harmful of all bugs. I think it's their size, combined with the fact that they spend most of their lives hidden under the earth — plus the husks; ohgod those husks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually intended that last log to leave room open to interpret that no one was sacrificed at all. A previous iteration made the sacrifice explicit, but here, the only clue any sacrifice has happened is that parents are standing behind their children and we get some reference to 'lambs slaughtered to feed the lion, the Son (Jesus) slaughtered to feed <blank>'.

That being said, I wasn't sure how else to end this — I needed things to escalate, I didn't want another sermon (it starts to drag a little I feel like), and I needed to clarify that his congregation is transforming into SCP-3089-A instances… and they're digging for some reason. The [UNINTELLIGIBLE] is meant to be their hissing and chittering.

ETA: Also, thank you for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like ending the sermon sections with:
"(Mildly cultish but still realistic stuff)"
UNINTELLIGIBLE
"Then you sacrifice your son"
Is a bit trite. We see that sort of thing all the time and I certainly feel the DATA EXPUNGED fatigue.

That said, everything after that feels fresh and is really cool. Also in the earlier sermons you hit that prosperity gospel feel right on the head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I should have more to say about this, and I will. But I just need it noted that I really like this. I can tell that you, too, are freaked out by cicadas, and it really helps you kinda capture the cicada god's wild moves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! And I will refrain from reposting, if only to avoid the wrath of the Downvote Gods.

Also, thank you for the feedback! I made a few changes based on what you pointed out:

Changes
– hide block
  • You're probably right about the cross-links, but I kind of wanted to be upfront about this, rather than cagey; I didn't want the reader to feel like the twist was that it was the Cicada God — I wanted the reader to know it was the Cicada God from Page 1, and for the twist to be that the PUAs and Tai Lopezes of the world are the Cicada God's blessed, Chosen Champions. That being said: I feel like if you don't click on those links, the piece works just fine.
  • The 'His Children' bit is something I thoughtlessly changed on a revision edit because I realized it was kind of incorrect (since he's speaking to a crowd), but you're absolutely right. On top of that, I now distinctly remember that the bit I stole this from (with Osteen) had him using the singular. So, changed!
  • Re: "Coughing up cicadas" — the PUAs are supposed to be anomalous, but I agree that it's a little… '?!?!'. It also feels like 'spoopy for spoopy's sake' to me, in rereading it. I've changed it to something more low-key (the women start chittering and hissing). I like this more because it also implies that by using it, you're possibly transforming women into SCP-3089-A instances, which is way more fucking horrible than making them vomit up cicadas.

Finally, re: Kafka's Parking Garage — I think part of it is that bugs terrify me, but part of it is also that in my mind, I see being transformed to a bug as being reduced to the lowest thing possible; you're just a few steps away from bacteria. To crudely steal from another religion's motif: You're at the bottom of the karmic food-chain. You're a fucking insect.

In a sense, that's kind of what I happening here: In their desperation to acquire various forms of success, the SCP-3089-A instances have sacrificed everything — their bodies, their minds, their spirituality — and have surrendered their identities up to a vast and terrible intelligence they don't comprehend.

This is pretty much what's happening in SCP-3074, too. The protagonist is so desperate to win the approval of this perfect stranger that they sacrifice everything about themselves — their independence, their will, their physical body, their very identity — it seems very logical to me that they would then be reduced to a chittering, scuttling insect.

I guess what I'm saying is that while I don't know if the two skips are directly related, the creatures at the bottom of Kafka's Parking Garage would definitely get along with instances of SCP-3090-A — and the voice on the phone probably has the Cicada God's number on speed-dial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you — I really appreciate it!

Dialogue is something that comes pretty naturally to me; probably because I enjoy listening to people and breaking apart how they communicate. I've been experimenting with trying to increase the verisimilitude of my dialogue (because yeah, dialogue definitely doesn't sound anything like how we write it, or portray it on TV — it's full of odd pauses, interruptions, repetitions, interjected affirmations, etc) while trying not to sacrifice its readability or flow. I think real dialogue is actually pretty boring to read (sometimes bordering on incomprehensible!) but there's a happy medium between 'real' and 'fictitious' that I'm trying to tap into, particularly recently — I'm very glad to hear that it's working!

(Also, thanks for the fix/noticing that — the PUA is meant to say Cicadetta montana, not Cicada montana — fixed!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

This is the third time I'm reading this, and the third time I'm commenting on it, and the third time I'm upvoting it. I swear if I see it again, I'm downvoting it. Anyway, as I read through this (for the third time) I will make comments.

+ Show
- Hide

(I/O-SILVER)

That really is a super clever name for a bot.

the most prominent of which is a fixation on achieving material success via the application of rituals associated with the Cétlaidí, or "Singers" (a Druidic cult active in Ireland between the 15th and 19th centuries).

By bringing these crosslinks up so soon, you are playing your had a bit early here. However, I do feel that this is unique enough to stand on its own, and unique enough In-Unvierse to be its own separate phenomenon.

Maybe you've heard that the Bible only nourishes the spirit; that God provides only for our immortal souls. Well, my friends, I am here to tell you that this is simply not true."

As before, your Bible-thumper passages are SPOT ON. I actually read these in Peter Popoff's voice, and it works quite well.

You are His children. You are His champions.

During one iteration, I believe you had it singular. "You are His child. You are His Champion." As someone who was born as raised religious (but no longer) that really resonated with me. For the briefest moment, I thought to myself, "This is speaking to me. I -am- his Champion." Having it plural dulls the impact for me.

Each of you, take one, and hold it in your left hand. Yes. The left hand, not the right. Your left.

Bits like this make it feel so very real. Like it's really a transcript of some guy in front of a crowd, giving directions.

"…lamb is slaughtered to feed the lion. The Son is slaughtered to feed…"

This little passage stands out to me as being especially disturbing.

chittering

So, did you decide on a head-canon where this fits with the Kafka Garage after all?

Ladders exist so you can climb them. That's what ladders are for, people! […] If you're not going for the top of that ladder, then this video isn't for you. Bye! Sorry to have wasted your time. Successful people don't deal with losers. Only keep watching if you want to achieve complete mastery over your life."

Again, you really hit the nail on the head with this transcript. This sounds EXACTLY like one of those motivational talks for dumb people.

They'll start coughing up cicadas and shit.

While I like how you change up the format with everything by making this two people conversing, this one line really pulls me out of it. Unless this is for an anamolous community, someone hearing that is going to wonder just what the hell is going on here. You might make it something like, "It'll start attracting cicadas to her. They'll show up in her clothing, in her dresser, in her bed… one time I heard of one trying to crawl up…"

All in all, an excellent piece to be proud of. I hope you are able to continue so the community can be enriched by more of your work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This dialogue is absolutely fantastic. There are some downsides to the piece – I feel the exposition-by-log only outlines this idea without filling in the potentially lovely details at the core, for instance – but this dialogue more than makes up for any gripes I have. The level of realism in the speech here is beyond gold-standard.

I'm gushing, and I'm going to provide some unnecessary backstory here, but I'm caught up in my enthusiasm and can't contain myself. I transcribed interviews for a summer job once, and it gave me this big insight into how actual spoken words look like when pressed down onto the page. All the repetitions, stumbling, sentence fragments, little affirmative interjections; all of those lovely imperfections that remind you of real life. After that, I've felt that all the speech I've since read in written fiction is lacking – like an abstraction, a Platonic theoretical, an idealised life drawing without the wrinkles and asymmetries of an actual human body.

But, damn, you captured it. Perfectly. That third log in particular – I could actually hear these guys in my head, I could hear their pauses, their cadences, their inflections; and that never, ever happens to me, because written dialogue is nearly always smooshed into the mould of narrative expediency, or simplistic characterisation, or whatever, and comes out looking like weird jelly. But this dialogue breathes, and moves, and gesticulates. It's not a puppet – it's a real boy.

I'm taking notes from this. I'm analysing how you conveyed interrupted speech, how the characters self-corrected previous sentences with more precise near-repetitions, people talking over each other, the lot. You may recall me being pretty snotty about Cragglewood – and I don't recant those comments, unfortunately! – but I'm bringing it up just to say that I don't dole out praise of this magnitude easily. I hope you can be really proud of the dialogue you've written here. Because you should be.

(Oh, one of the PUAs says Cicada montana instead of Cicadetta montana. Is that an intentional illustration that this brash dickwad is using powers beyond his ken without appreciating the details and consequences of doing so?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always amazed by how natural your dialogue is. Stay strong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Obviously still very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still liking this. Cicadas are of interest to me, as one of the famous insects I've never actually seen.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Oh, give me that old time religion
Give me that old time religion
Give me that old time religion

It's good enough for me~

SOURCES:

  • Images from here, here, here, and here.
  • Inspired/derived from kinchtheknifeblade's [SCP-3004 (Imago) and [SCP-2852 (Cousin Johnny).

FEEDBACK:

  • Weryllium - ([SCP-3435] - Dinosaurs and Dragons Care Not For Your Politics)
  • Tufto - ([SCP-3699] - Memoirs of a Shingle Beach)
  • mlister - ([SCP-2514] - Make-The-Tears-Go-Away Pony)
  • psul - ([SCP-2162] - … as normal as blueberry pie)
  • fieldstone - ([SCP-3661] - A Toy Story)
  • Decibelle - ([SCP-3290] - Righteous Punishment for Vampires)

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!
